question: Which papers contradict the hypothesis that central insulin resistance mediates GLP-1 receptor agonist neuroprotection in Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease?
note: Drawn entirely from papers already in literature_extraction.json and metabolic_subgroup_extraction.json — no new search performed. Organized by contradiction type, since 'contradicts' covers several distinct logical failure modes for a mediation claim.

contradiction_types.type: 1. Neuroprotection occurs WITHOUT insulin-pathway engagement (decoupling: effect without mechanism)
contradiction_types.why_this_contradicts_mediation: If insulin-resistance reversal is the mediator, blocking/absent change in insulin-pathway markers should track with absent neuroprotection. These papers show the opposite: full neuroprotective phenotype with a flat or null insulin-pathway marker.

papers.citation: Carranza-Naval et al. 2021 (Front Aging Neurosci) — APP/PS1xdb/db mice
papers.finding: Liraglutide reduced brain/cortical atrophy, amyloid plaque burden, tau hyperphosphorylation, microglial activation, and improved cognition (Morris water maze) — but 'no differences were observed in the cortex' for IR-A, IR-B, or IGF-1R mRNA expression between groups. This is the cleanest single counter-example in the entire literature set: the full neuroprotective package occurred with a flat insulin-receptor-pathway readout.

papers.citation: Paladugu et al. 2021 (Int J Mol Sci) — STZ/5xFAD mice
papers.finding: Liraglutide restored IDE, pIR, and pGSK3β, but explicitly 'did not significantly elevate pAkt levels' — the central node of the proposed IR-IRS-1-PI3K-Akt-GSK3β chain failed to move. Separately, despite the biochemical changes that did occur, there was 'no significant difference' in degenerating neurons (FJB staining) or behavioral/cognitive measures — i.e., the biochemistry moved without the phenotype moving, in the same study.

papers.citation: Gejl et al. 2016 (Front Aging Neurosci) — Liraglutide AD RCT
papers.finding: Cerebral glucose metabolism (CMRglc) decline was prevented by liraglutide in a population that was explicitly non-diabetic and normoglycemic at baseline (fasting glucose ~5.6-5.8 mmol/L, HbA1c ~5.5-5.6% in both arms — no insulin resistance to reverse). A neuroprotective-adjacent signal (CMRglc preservation) occurred in the absence of any baseline insulin resistance, which is hard to square with 'reversing insulin resistance' as the operative mechanism in this trial specifically.

contradiction_types.type: 2. The literal proposed central marker fails, even as some clinical benefit appears elsewhere
contradiction_types.why_this_contradicts_mediation: Cerebral glucose metabolism is the most direct available clinical proxy for central insulin/glucose signaling. If this is null while other benefits appear via a different readout (volume, cognition), the mechanism driving the clinical benefit is more likely something other than restored central insulin signaling.

papers.citation: Edison et al. 2025 / ELAD (Nat Med) — Liraglutide AD phase 2b
papers.finding: Primary outcome (cerebral glucose metabolism via PET) was NOT significant (p=0.14) — the trial's literal designed readout of central insulin/glucose signaling failed. The cognitive benefit that was observed (ADAS-Exec, p=0.01) and the reduced brain volume loss occurred alongside this null metabolic result, suggesting these benefits may not be running through the glucose-metabolism/insulin-signaling channel the trial was built to detect.

papers.citation: Cummings et al. 2026 / evoke + evoke+ (Lancet) — Oral semaglutide AD phase 3
papers.finding: Completely null on the primary endpoint (CDR-SB) in both trials, despite being the largest trial (n=3,808) and the only one with a substantial diabetic/metabolically-impaired subgroup (13.6%, n~516) — exactly the population that should show a signal first if the mechanism were real and clinically relevant. A null result in the best-powered, most metabolically-diverse trial in this literature is a meaningful negative data point against the mechanism mattering clinically, at least at this dose/duration/disease-stage.

contradiction_types.type: 3. Direct empirical test of 'metabolic status predicts response' — found non-significant
contradiction_types.why_this_contradicts_mediation: This is the most direct possible test of the hypothesis at the patient level, and it came back negative.

papers.citation: Athauda et al. 2019 post-hoc analysis (Eur J Neurosci) — Exenatide-PD trial
papers.finding: Explicitly tested BMI and insulin resistance as candidate predictors of motor response to exenatide in PD. Neither was among the significant predictors. Tremor-dominant phenotype and milder baseline disease (lower MDS-UPDRS Part 2, shorter disease duration) predicted benefit instead — phenotype/disease-stage factors, not metabolic factors.

contradiction_types.type: 4. Pooled/meta-analytic clinical evidence is null or very-low-certainty, undermining confidence that ANY mechanism (insulin-resistance-mediated or otherwise) reliably translates clinically
contradiction_types.why_this_contradicts_mediation: If the proposed mechanism reliably drove clinical benefit, you'd expect convergent, reasonably consistent positive findings across trials/agents. Pooled analyses show the opposite for both diseases.

papers.citation: Santos et al. 2026 (Pharmaceutics) — meta-analysis, liraglutide/exenatide in AD/MCI, 3 RCTs, n=278
papers.finding: Pooled cognitive outcome (ADAS-Cog/MMSE) SMD = -0.21 (95% CI -0.81 to 0.38) — not significant. Conclusion: 'current randomized evidence does not support a meaningful improvement in cognitive outcomes with GLP-1RAs in individuals with AD or MCI due to AD.'

papers.citation: Helal et al. 2025 (Diabetol Metab Syndr) — meta-analysis, GLP-1RAs in PD, 5 trials, n=580
papers.finding: Of 6 pooled outcome measures, only one (MDS-UPDRS III ON-medication, endpoints) reached significance (p=0.01); all others (midpoints, OFF-medication, Part II, Part IV, Non-Motor Symptoms Scale) were non-significant. Conclusion explicitly states results were 'inconclusive' and currently 'not recommended... outside of research contexts.'

papers.citation: Mulvaney et al. 2020 (Cochrane Database Syst Rev) — GLP-1RAs in PD
papers.finding: GRADE certainty rated low-to-very-low throughout, based on only 2 small trials (107 participants total). The highest-rigor evidence-grading body available rates this entire literature as weak — which doesn't contradict a true effect existing, but does contradict treating the mechanism as established.

contradiction_types.type: 5. Drug-superiority differences attributed to a non-insulin mechanism (pharmacokinetics, not pathway engagement)
contradiction_types.why_this_contradicts_mediation: When researchers explain why one GLP-1-class drug outperforms another, insulin-pathway engagement is often not the explanation offered — undercutting insulin signaling as the operative differentiator.

papers.citation: Zhang et al. 2023 (Parkinsons Dis) — DA5-CH vs liraglutide, A53T mice
papers.finding: DA5-CH outperformed liraglutide on nearly every measured parameter (motor function, alpha-synuclein, cytokines, autophagy, mitochondrial morphology) — but the paper attributes this to DA5-CH's 'better blood-brain barrier penetration,' not to differential insulin-signaling engagement. No insulin-signaling marker was actually measured in this study at all, despite it being framed as relevant to brain insulin desensitization in the introduction.

contradiction_types.type: 6. Canonical curated pathway databases do not encode this mechanism as established
contradiction_types.why_this_contradicts_mediation: Not a 'contradiction' in the data sense, but a structural one: the field's own curated reference (KEGG) for these diseases doesn't include insulin-signaling components, indicating the broader scientific community has not yet validated this as settled mechanism.

papers.citation: KEGG hsa05010 (Alzheimer disease pathway) and hsa05012 (Parkinson disease pathway)
papers.finding: Neither curated disease pathway map includes insulin receptor, IRS-1, PI3K, Akt, or GSK-3β as pathway components, despite both maps being actively maintained and detailed on other mechanisms (calcium signaling, ER stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, AGE-RAGE).

contradiction_types.type: 7. Large observational effect sizes are inconsistent with null RCTs for the same disease/drug class — a classic confounding signature, not confirmatory evidence
contradiction_types.why_this_contradicts_mediation: If insulin-resistance reversal genuinely drove a strong, real neuroprotective effect, you would expect RCTs to detect at least a modest version of what large observational studies show. Instead, the gap between huge observational hazard ratios and null RCTs in the same disease area is the textbook signature of healthy-user/indication bias in the observational data — meaning those large effect sizes likely overstate any true causal (insulin-resistance-mediated or otherwise) effect, rather than confirming the mechanism.

papers.citation: AbuAlrob et al. 2025 (real-world cohort) — HR 0.30 for dementia incidence
papers.finding: vs. the same-disease RCT evidence (Cummings 2026 evoke/evoke+, completely null; Santos 2026 meta-analysis, null). A 70% relative risk reduction in observational data alongside a null RCT in the same population/disease is discordant in a way that should lower confidence in the observational estimate, not raise confidence in the mechanism.

papers.citation: Wang et al. 2025 (real-world target trial emulation, T2D population) — HR 0.54-0.80 for ADRD vs other antidiabetics
papers.finding: Strong relative benefit, but entirely within a diabetic (already metabolically-defined) population comparing drugs against each other — cannot be used to argue insulin-resistance reversal specifically drives the benefit, since all comparators also act on insulin/glucose pathways to varying degrees; the comparison conflates drug-specific and insulin-pathway-general effects.

summary.strongest_single_contradiction: Carranza-Naval et al. 2021 — full AD/diabetes-model neuroprotective phenotype (atrophy, amyloid, tau, cognition all improved) with a completely flat insulin-receptor-pathway mRNA readout in the same animals. This is the cleanest within-study dissociation of effect from proposed mechanism in the whole literature set.
summary.strongest_clinical_contradiction: Athauda et al. 2019 post-hoc analysis — the one study that directly tested 'does metabolic/insulin-resistance status predict who benefits more' and found the answer was no (phenotype and disease-stage predicted response instead).
summary.overall_pattern: Across preclinical studies, insulin-pathway markers and neuroprotective/behavioral outcomes frequently move independently of each other (sometimes pathway moves without phenotype, sometimes phenotype moves without pathway). Across clinical trials, the trials with literal central-insulin/glucose-metabolism endpoints (Gejl 2016, ELAD 2025) most often show null results on that specific endpoint even when other benefits appear. And the largest, most metabolically-diverse trial (evoke/evoke+) was completely null. None of this disproves a role for central insulin resistance — but it is a substantial, multi-pronged body of evidence that the simple 'GLP-1RA -> restores central insulin signaling -> neuroprotection' causal chain is not well supported as the dominant or sole explanatory mechanism, and may not be necessary for the (often modest) clinical benefits that do appear.
